focus_areas: Fix join ordering: apply highest-selectivity filter FIRST (c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING', 22% selectivity) to minimize intermediate rows

Two-phase strategy: (1) filter customer on mktsegment (22% â†’ ~330K rows), (2) join with filtered orders (o_orderdate < '1995-03-15', 33% selectivity on unfiltered â†’ ~5M orders matched to ~330K customers)

For the 3-way join (customerâ†’ordersâ†’lineitem): build hash table on filtered customer.c_custkey (330K rows, fits in L3 cache), probe with filtered orders, then probe result with lineitem on l_orderkey
Use radix partitioning on l_orderkey if join intermediate exceeds memory budget (partition into 64 subpartitions, each ~1M rows)
Hash index on c_mktsegment (already in storage design) enables efficient customer filtering
Sorted index on o_custkey (already in storage design) enables efficient customerâ†’orders join
Sorted index on l_orderkey (already in storage design) enables efficient ordersâ†’lineitem join
Parallelize filtered scans with morsel-driven execution (64 cores)
Final aggregation: group by (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) â†’ ~100K groups; use hash aggregation with partial agg per thread
TOP-K optimization: use heap sort on 10 rows (sort_topk), not full sort on 100K rows
strategy_notes: Q3 is the critical bottleneck (386ms vs 91ms DuckDB). Root cause: multi-way join materialization and suboptimal join ordering. Apply filters (mktsegment, orderdate) BEFORE building join hash tables to reduce intermediate cardinality. Use existing indexes on c_mktsegment, o_custkey, l_orderkey aggressively. Parallelize filtered scans. For the final step (aggregate + TOP-K), use hash aggregation with thread-local tables, then heap sort on 10 rows. Target: <150ms (competitive with DuckDB).
reasoning: TPC-H SF10 workload dominated by 60M-row lineitem scans across three distinct patterns. Q1 and Q6 already achieve competitive performance; Q3's 3-way join (customerâ†’ordersâ†’lineitem) is 4.2x slower than DuckDB (386ms vs 91ms). Critical optimizations: (1) Exploit 64-core parallelism with morsel-driven execution; (2) Fix Q3's join materialization bottleneck through optimal join ordering and radix partitioning; (3) Leverage existing storage design (zone maps, sorted indexes, dictionary encoding) with aggressive filter pushdown. Hardware supports AVX512 vectorization for filtering and aggregation.
